summary: DeVault argues that qemu is the only hypervisor worth using, dismissing VirtualBox, VMWare, and other alternatives as inferior. He acknowledges qemu's steep learning curve due to its extensive options but walks readers through a basic Linux VM setup using Alpine Linux. The tutorial covers creating virtual disks, configuring KVM acceleration, networking with virtio, and display output. He frames qemu's flexibility as both its greatest strength and weakness, comparing it to ffmpeg as a swiss army knife tool. The post closes with a list of advanced qemu capabilities and a characteristically blunt dismissal of all competing hypervisors.
key_points: qemu is superior to all other hypervisors in speed, portability, guest support, and features
qemu's extensive option set makes it intimidating for beginners but provides unmatched flexibility
virtio drivers provide significantly faster disk and network performance than emulated hardware
qemu can do everything from simple desktop VMs to GPU passthrough, architecture emulation, and remote display via SPICE
Both qemu and ffmpeg were created by Fabrice Bellard, sharing a swiss-army-knife philosophy
money_quotes: There's no excuse to use anything other than qemu, in my books.
qemu's greatest strength is also its greatest weakness: it has so many options that it's hard to know which ones you need just to get started.
qemu is the swiss army knife of virtualisation, much like ffmpeg is the swiss army knife of multimedia.
qemu is fast, portable, better supported by guests, and has more features than Hollywood.
There's nothing other hypervisors can do that qemu can't, and there's plenty qemu can that they cannot.
They're all dogshit compared to qemu.
If you use VirtualBox after reading this article you make poor life choices and are an embarrassment to us all.
key_insight: qemu's intimidating complexity masks the fact that a basic VM setup requires only a handful of options, and its unmatched flexibility makes every other hypervisor obsolete.
